title: Political and legal
summary: 9609 A Level — political stability, regulation, trade policy, and legal compliance for business strategy.
learningObjectives: Analyse political factors affecting business decisions.
Explain impact of legal and regulatory frameworks.
Evaluate compliance as barrier or competitive advantage.
Apply political/legal analysis to case firms.

simpleExplanation.title: Government and the law
simpleExplanation.summary: Governments set the rules of business — taxes, trade barriers, employment law, and product safety standards all shape strategy and costs.
steps: Political stability encourages or deters investment.
Health, safety, and consumer protection law.
Compliance can exclude rivals or raise costs.

flashcards.back: Stable government → confidence to invest; instability → delay projects, currency risk.
Minimum wage, working time limits, anti-discrimination, health and safety duties.
Product safety standards, accurate advertising, refund rights.
Tax on imports — raises cost of imported inputs or products.
High standards may deter smaller rivals; build trust with customers.
PESTLE informs strategic choice — enter/avoid markets based on legal risk.

sections.content: **Political and legal** (6.1.1) opens **Topic 6 PESTLE** (A Level). Use **named policies/laws** from the case country — generic "government may change law" scores few marks.

items: **Political:** election outcomes, trade agreements, industrial policy, corruption risk.
**Legal:** company law, contract law, IP protection, environmental regulation.
**Impact on marketing:** advertising standards, data protection (GDPR).
**Impact on operations:** H&S inspections, planning permission.

sections.question: A UK food exporter sells to Country X, which imposes a 25% import tariff on processed foods. Analyse two business impacts.
sections.solution:
**1. Pricing/competitiveness:** Tariff raises **landed cost** 25% — either **absorb** (lower margin) or **raise price** (lose volume to local producers). May need **local production** (6.1.6) to avoid tariff.

**2. Strategic uncertainty:** **Trade policy** may change with elections — long-term **investment** in Country X riskier; firm may **diversify markets** or lobby for **free trade agreement**.
